coredata.dc:title: Development and application of tricolor ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on molecularly imprinted nanoparticles for visual detection of dibutyl phthalate in seawater and fish samples
coredata.dc:creator: You, Jin-Jie
Liu, Hua
Zhang, Rong-Rong
Pan, Qiao-Fen
Sun, Ai-Li
Zhang, Ze-Ming
Shi, Xi-Zhi
coredata.dc:description: A tricolor ratiometric fluorescence sensor was fabricated by mixing blue- and red-emission molecularly imprinted quantum dots (MIP-QDs) with green-emission quantum dots at the optimal ratio. The MIP-QDs were synthesized by coating CdSe/ZnS QDs in polymer by inverse microemulsion method. Compared with single-emission or dual-emission sensors, the tricolor ratiometric fluorescence sensor provided a wider range of color variations for visual DBP detection. The ratio fluorescence value I530/(I450 + I630) of the tricolor ratiometric fluorescence sensor linearly changed within the concentration of 2.0–20.0 × 103 μg/L DBP. The correlation coefficient was 0.9910, and the limits of detection were 1.0 μg/kg and 0.65 μg/L in fish and seawater, respectively. Meanwhile, the fluorescence color gradually changed from purple to plum to pink to salmon to yellowish green and finally to green. The recoveries of DBP in fish and seawater were 84.3 %–91.4 % and 88.3 %–110.3 %, respectively. Moreover, no obvious differences were observed between the detection results of the tricolor ratiometric fluorescence sensor and gas chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry. The tricolor ratiometric fluorescence sensor constructed herein provides an ideal choice for rapid and intuitive DBP detection in environmental and aquatic products.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Molecularly imprinted quantum dots
Tricolor
Ratiometric fluorescence
Visual detection
Dibutyl phthalate

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication and characterization of the MIP-QDs
Effects of key factors on the fluorescence properties of the fluorescence sensor
Fluorescence quenching mechanism of the MIP-QDs
Practical applicability of the developed sensor

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was funded by the Key Research and Development Program of Zhejiang Province ( 2022C02028 , 2021C02062 ), the Natural Science Foundation of Zhejiang Province ( LGF21C190001 , LQ21C200002 ), Ningbo Science and Technology Bureau ( 202002N3066 , 2022S163 ) and Major Scientific and Technological Project of Ningbo ( 2021Z102 , 2021Z056 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Effects of (A) the concentration of MIP-red-QDs and (B) percentage of water in ethanol and (C) the pH of the system and (D) the salinity of the system on the fluorescence response of the MIP/NIP-red-QDs.

Tricolor system optimization. (A–H) Vblue-MIP:VGQDs:Vred-MIP = 2:10:8, 4:10:8, 6:10:8, 8:10:8, 10:10:8, 12:10:8, 14:10:8, and 16:10:8. The left insets are the corresponding fluorescence images with increasing DBP addition from on to down.
(A and C) Selectivity and competitiveness of a single-emission sensor. (B and D) Selectivity and competitiveness of the TRF sensor. (Cstructural analogue = 3CDBP; DBP concentration was 5 mg/L).

(A and B) Fluorescence spectra of the TRF sensor based on MIP-QDs and the TRF sensor based on NIP-QDs at different DBP concentrations. (a and b) Standard curves of the TRF sensor based on MIP-QDs to DBP and of the TRF sensor based on NIP-QDs to DBP (the following insets are fluorescence color cards).
The determination of DBP in fish and seawater samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Development and application of tricolor ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on molecularly imprinted nanoparticles for visual detection of dibutyl phthalate in seawater and fish samples

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory for Managing Biotic and Chemical Threats to the Quality and Safety of Agro-products, School of Marine Sciences, Ningbo University, Ningbo 315211, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory for Managing Biotic and Chemical Threats to the Quality and Safety of Agro-products

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory for Managing Biotic and Chemical Threats to the Quality and Safety of Agro-products, School of Marine Sciences, Ningbo University, Ningbo, 315211, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Marine Sciences, Ningbo University, Ningbo 315211, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Marine Sciences, Ningbo University, Ningbo 315211, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food and Pharmaceutical Sciences, Ningbo University, Ningbo, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food and Pharmaceutical Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Food and Pharmaceutical Sciences, Ningbo University, Ningbo, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: School of Marine Sciences, Ningbo University, 818 Fenghua Road, Ningbo 315211, China.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A tricolor ratiometric fluorescence sensor was fabricated by mixing blue- and red-emission molecularly imprinted quantum dots (MIP-QDs) with green-emission quantum dots at the optimal ratio. The MIP-QDs were synthesized by coating CdSe/ZnS QDs in polymer by inverse microemulsion method. Compared with single-emission or dual-emission sensors, the tricolor ratiometric fluorescence sensor provided a wider range of color variations for visual DBP detection. The ratio fluorescence value I530/(I450 + I630) of the tricolor ratiometric fluorescence sensor linearly changed within the concentration of 2.0–20.0 × 103 μg/L DBP. The correlation coefficient was 0.9910, and the limits of detection were 1.0 μg/kg and 0.65 μg/L in fish and seawater, respectively. Meanwhile, the fluorescence color gradually changed from purple to plum to pink to salmon to yellowish green and finally to green. The recoveries of DBP in fish and seawater were 84.3 %–91.4 % and 88.3 %–110.3 %, respectively. Moreover, no obvious differences were observed between the detection results of the tricolor ratiometric fluorescence sensor and gas chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry. The tricolor ratiometric fluorescence sensor constructed herein provides an ideal choice for rapid and intuitive DBP detection in environmental and aquatic products.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Phthalic acid esters (PAEs), which are monomeric plasticizers, are mainly used to improve the durability and flexibility of plastic products (Cao, 2010; Paluselli et al., 2018) [1,2]. As a predominant PAE congener and a major type of PAE pollutant in the environment, dibutyl phthalate (DBP) and its metabolites have been detected in the water environment and biological samples in large quantities (Lian et al., 2014; Net et al., 2015; Zhang et al., 2015; Hu et al., 2020). DBP is also considered an endocrine disruptor that has a strong reproductive toxicity and carcinogenicity and teratogenicity (Bajkin et al., 2014). The United States, European Union and China have listed DBP as a priority pollutant in environmental testing (Lu et al., 2020). The methods currently used in detecting DBP are gas chromatography coupled with mass spectrometry (GC–MS), enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay, high-performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry, and surface plasmon resonance biosensors (Wu et al., 2014; Park et al., 2014; Gonzalez-Salamo et al., 2018; Zhou et al., 2019). However, these methods require long pretreatment and detection takes a long time. Moreover, they are greatly influenced by environmental matrixes and have a low sensitivity. Therefore, a simple, rapid, and highly selective analytical method for detecting DBP in complex samples must be developed.

Molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) are biomimetic materials that have imprinting cavities complementary to the shape and size of the target molecule. Aside from the favorable selectivity and specificity of biological antibodies, MIPs also have excellent mechanical stability, rapid equilibrium kinetics, and good reusability (Whitcombe et al., 2011). When the high selectivity of MIPs is combined with the high sensitivity of fluorescence detection, the obtained MIPs not only have a regular morphology, which is conducive to chemical modification, but also have an excellent fluorescence detection performance (Wang et al., 2018). Therefore, the development of fluorescence sensors based on MIPs has attracted increased attention. Among various fluorescent signal materials, quantum dots (QDs) are the most popular because of their bright photoluminescence, adjustable luminous color, narrow luminous range, and high optical stability (Yu et al., 2017). Some research groups have successfully developed fluorescence sensors based on MIP-QDs, which have a high selectivity and a sensitive response to the target analyte (Mehrzad-Samarin et al., 2017; Shabnam and Hajar, 2021). However, this single-emission mode can only exhibit the variations of fluorescence brightness by either the “turn on” or the “turn off” mode with the analyte, a feature that greatly limits the quantitative capability of MIP-QDs (Cai et al., 2018; Lee et al., 2015). Moreover, their fluorescence color window is single, their visual effect is poor, and tiny changes in fluorescence brightness are difficult to capture by the naked eye (Yang et al., 2018; Bigdeli et al., 2019). As a new type of sensor, MIP ratiometric fluorescence sensors have a higher sensitivity and a better self-correction function than single-emission fluorescence sensors, which can avoid biological background and environmental interference (Feng et al., 2017; Konwar et al., 2019). Dual/multiple fluorescence emissions increase the color change window of the sensor and improve the sensitivity and accuracy of visual detection (Yang et al., 2018; Tawfik et al., 2020). However, the color variation provided by dual-emission sensors is still limited within a certain range (Li et al., 2017). Regardless of which of the two fluorescent colors are involved, the most extensive color changes are in their transition colors, but the range of color changes remains limited (Cai et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2018; Amjadi and Jalili, 2017). The tricolor emission system based on the red–green–blue fluorescence mode can expand the range of fluorescence color variations and display more colorful fluorescence colors in the spectral range compared with the dual-emission fluorescence system, thereby further broadening the design and application of MIP ratiometric fluorescent sensors. In practical application, the trichromatic emission system can further improve the detection accuracy of the sensor and reduce detection error through self-calibration. Meanwhile, different fluorescent color windows provide rich color changes for visual detection (Yang et al., 2018; Tawfik et al., 2020). To the best of our knowledge, no reports exist in the literature on the application of MIP-QD tricolor emission fluorescence sensors in detecting environmental pollutants. On the basis of the principle of MIP tricolor-emission fluorescence sensing, we proposed a novel MIP tricolor ratiometric fluorescence (TRF) sensor that is accompanied by broad and profuse fluorescence color change from purple to green for the rapid visual detection of DBP. The integration of MIP-QDs into the sensor improved its detection sensitivity, reduced its detection time, and produced rich fluorescence color changes. Results indicated that the sensor has a great application prospect in the visual detection of seawater and biological samples.

Triton X-100 was bought from Solarbio Co. Ltd. (Beijing, China). (3-Aminopropyl)triethoxysilane (APTES, 99 %) and tetraethoxysilane (TEOS, 99 %) were supplied by Sigma-Aldrich (Steinheim, Germany). DBP, bis-(2-ethylhexyl) phthalate (DEHP), butyl benzyl phthalate (BBP), diethyl phthalate (DEP), dinonyl phthalate (DNP), and di-isobutyl phthalate (DiBP) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (>98 % purity; St. Louis, MO, USA). CdSe/ZnS QDs (emission wavelengths: 450, 530, and 630 nm) were procured from Beida Jubang Science and Technology Co. Ltd. (Beijing, China). Analytical-grade cyclohexane, ammonium hydroxide (25 wt%), ethanol, and acetonitrile were acquired from Sinopharm Group Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ultrapure water (18.25 MΩ cm) was filtered by a water clarifier (Sichuan, China).

The MIP-QDs for DBP detection were synthesized via a modified reverse microemulsion method (Xiao et al., 2016). In brief, 7.5 mL of cyclohexanel and 1.8 mL of Triton X-100 were added into a 50 mL round-bottom flask, and magnetically stirred at 400 r/min for 15 min. Then, 200 μL (2.5 mg/mL) of QDs, 40 μL TEOS and 100 μL ammonium hydroxide (25 wt%) were successively added and stirred at 400 r/min for 2 h. Meanwhile, the solution containing 200 μL DBP (20 mg/mL), 200 μL cyclohexane, and 25.6 μL APTES was prepared by sufficient stirring at 400 r/min for 2 h, and subsequently added into the flask and reacted for 12 h. After polymerization, 10 mL acetone was added and ultrasonicated for 5 min for demulsification. After centrifugation at 8.5 × 103 g for 10 min, the synthesized polymers were washed with ultrapure water to remove incompletely reacted APTES and TEOS. Finally, the template was removed with the eluent (Vethanol: Vacetonitrile, 8:2) by ultrasonication and shocking, until no more DBP was detected by LC-UV. Correspondingly, nonimprinted-QDs (NIP-QDs) were also synthesized without DBP.

The UV absorption was recorded using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (UV-3300, Mapada, China). Fluorescence intensity was recorded using an F-4600 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) and Varioskan Flash (Thermo Fisher Scientific, 3001/3001–1193). The shape and structure of the MIP-QDs and the NIP-QDs were examined via scanning electron microscopy (SEM, S-4800, Hitachi Japan), transmission electron microscopy (TEM, H-7650, Hitachi Japan), and scanning probe microscopy (Dimension ICON). Functional groups (4000–400 cm−1) were determined via Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (FT/IR-4700, Jasco). Elemental composition was detected via energy-dispersive spectroscopy (EDS, S-4800, Hitachi Japan). The fluorescence attenuation curve of the detection system was measured by a fluorescence spectrometer (FL3–111).

The fluorescence intensity of the MIP red/blue-QDs and GQDs was measured using an F-4600 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). The excitation wavelength was set to 345 nm, and the fluorescence emission intensity at 450, 530, and 630 nm was recorded. The slit width of the excitation and emission wavelengths was both set to 5 nm, and the voltage was set to 700 eV. The fluorescence intensity of bicolor and tricolor mixtures was measured using Varioskan Flash (Thermo Fisher Scientific, 3001/3001–1193) with a slit width of the excitation and emission wavelengths both 5 nm (λ ex = 345 nm, scanning range: 380–680 nm). All experiments were conducted in triplicates.

The main construction process was divided into two parts. Firstly, green-emission QDs (GQDs, Vacuum-dried at 60 °C for 12 h and dissolved in ethanol) and the MIP-red-QDs were completely dispersed in a specific solution (Vethanol: Vwater, 7:3) with a constant volume of 200 μL. After the solution was incubated for 5 min, the fluorescence intensity at 345 nm excitation wavelength was recorded. Subsequently, the fluorescence intensity was measured under a 365 nm UV lamp, and images were taken for RGB color extraction. Secondly, the MIP-blue-QDs were added and completely dispersed in a 200 μL specific solution (Vethanol: Vwater, 7:3) on the basis of dual-color optimization. The remaining operations were the same as those performed in the dual-emission system.

DBP and its structural analogs, including DEHP, BBP, DEP, DNP and DiBP, were selected to evaluate the selectivity of the developed sensors system. Fluorescence response properties were calculated according to the Stern–Volmer equation: F
0 /F = 1 + K
SV [Q], where F
0 and F are the initial fluorescence intensity and those after DBP addition for 5 min, respectively. In the TRF sensor, F
0 is the initial fluorescence ratio: I RGB (I RGB = I 530/[I 450 + I 630]), where I 450, I 530, and I 630 are the fluorescence intensity of the TRF sensor at 450, 530, and 630 nm, respectively; [Q] is the quencher concentration; and Ksv is the quenching constant of the quencher. Imprinting factor (IF) was defined as the K
SV ratio of the MIP-QDs and the NIP-QDs. Moreover, the fluorescence quenching efficiency was evaluated by the formula (F
0
F)/F
0 . Competitive experiments were conducted by mixing DBP with its structural analogs at concentrations three times higher than that of DBP.

Biological and seawater samples were collected from an aquaculture area in Xiangshan Bay. The biological samples were treated by the modified QuEChERS method (Zhang et al., 2021). In brief, 2.00 g (dry weight) of the biological samples was weighed and put into a 10 mL polytetrafluoroethylene centrifuge tube, and then 200 mg anhydrous MgSO4 and 5 mL acetonitrile were added. After ultrasonication for 20 min, the samples were centrifuged at 9.0 × 103 g for 10 min. These steps were repeated two times, and the supernatants were combined. Then, 150 mg N-propylenediamine (PSA), 150 mg C18, and 100 mg graphitized carbon black (GCB) were added by shaking for 5 min. Finally, the purified extract was placed in a glass test tube for nitrogen blowing to near dry, followed by filtration using a 0.22 μm organic hydrophobic filter membrane after constant volume with ethanol. The extract was stored in a glass vial for GC–MS/MS detection (Zhang et al., 2021). The seawater samples were analyzed via headspace solid-phase microextraction for GC–MS/MS analysis (Zhang et al., 2021), and the seawater samples were analyzed by tricolor ratiometric fluorescence sensor after filtration. The instrument testing method is described in detail in the Supporting Material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and characterization of the MIP-QDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The amount of TEOS and the ratio of the template to APTES largely affect the imprinting effect and the specific response of MIP-QDs to the template (Chang et al., 2013). As shown in Fig. S1, when TEOS was 40 μL and the ratio of APTES to DBP was 6:1, the highest IF value of the MIP-red-QDs was obtained. Moreover, the F
0 /F value of the NIP-red-QDs prepared was lower than that of the MIP-red-QDs. These results demonstrated that the MIP-red-QDs exhibited excellent recognition effect and fluorescence quenching ability for DBP. The MIP-blue-QDs were also fabricated following the same procedure in addition to blue-CdSe/ZnS QDs.

The morphology of the MIP-QDs and GQDs was characterized via SEM and TEM. The MIP-blue-QDs and the MIP-red-QDs had a spherical core-shell structure and an average particle size of approximately 50 nm (Fig. S2). TEM showed that the NIP-QDs had a smooth surface and a uniform spherical shape, whereas the MIP-QDs had a rough surface. Meanwhile, atomic force microscope images showed holes on the MIP-QDs' surface, whereas the NIP-QDs had a completely smooth surface, indicating that numerous imprinted cavities formed on the MIP-QDs' surface. Furthermore, the elements of the MIP-QDs were analyzed via EDS. Basically the same element types were found in the MIP-QDs and NIP-QDs (Fig. S3). The elements Si and O came from the SiO2 layer provided by TEOS and APTES, whereas the elements Cd, Se, Zn, and S came from the CdSe/ZnS QDs. The element Pt came from gold spraying. The element N was provided by the amino group in APTES. The elements Si and O were found in the MIP-QDs and the NIP-QDs, indicating the existence of SiO2 matrices. The GQDs (e) contained only the elements Cd, Se, Zn, and S. The core–shell structure and the results of the elemental analysis demonstrated that SiO2 imprinting layers were successfully grafted onto the QDs' surface.

The FTIR spectra of the MIP-QDs are illustrated in Fig. S4. The vibration peaks near 791 and 465 cm−1 were derived from the stretching vibration of SiO. The characteristic peak at 1061 cm−1 was the stretching vibration of Si–O–Si, indicating that the silicon sphere was successfully formed. The stretching vibrations at 2935 cm−1 were generally considered to be from –CH3 and –CH2 vibrations. Furthermore, the stretching vibration peaks at 3368, 1652, and 1563 cm−1 were from the –NH/NH2 chemical bond. However, DBP has no –NH/NH2 bond, suggesting that the –NH/NH2 bond from APTES was successfully grafted onto the surface of the QDs (Zhou et al., 2014; Liu et al., 2020). The results indicated that the QDs were successfully coated onto the silica spheres, and APTES was also successfully grafted onto the surface of the QDs. The MIP-QDs and the NIP-QDs shared similar primary compositions, indicating that their major backbones had no obvious differences.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of key factors on the fluorescence properties of the fluorescence sensor
ce:section.ce:para:
Conventionally, sufficient amounts of MIP-QDs will lead to an excellent linear range of sensor detection, whereas excessive amounts will reduce the sensitivity of DBP detection (Wei et al., 2015). As shown in Fig. 1A, the fluorescence quenching response (F
0
F) of the MIP-QDs to DBP gradually increased with the increase of the MIP-red-QDs concentration, and the highest fluorescence quenching efficiency was obtained at the MIP-red-QDs concentration of 20 mg/L. Meanwhile, a sufficient interaction between imprinting sites and analytes is needed to obtain the best fluorescence quenching response. After 5 mg/L DBP was added, the fluorescence intensity of the sensor reached equilibrium at 300 s (Fig. S5). Therefore, in subsequent experiments, the concentration of the MIP-QDs was set to 20.0 mg/L, and the optimal response time was set to 300 s.

Furthermore, the specific fluorescence quenching reaction of MIP-QDs is affected by the imprinted cavities space configuration of the imprinted silicon layer (Zhou et al., 2017). As shown in Fig. 1B, the presence of water considerably affected the DBP selectivity of the MIP-red-QDs, whereas only small variations were observed in the NIP-red-QDs. The MIP-red-QDs had the best fluorescence response to DBP when the proportion of water was 30 %, and the IF value reached 2.21. These results confirmed that the hydrophobic interaction and the complementary specific recognition of the MIP-red-QDs to DBP had a remarkable effect on selective fluorescence quenching response (Zhang et al., 2020; Xiao et al., 2016). Therefore, the proportion of water in the system was set to 30 % in subsequent experiments. Furthermore, as pH increased, the fluorescence quenching ability (F
0 /F) of the MIP-red-QDs to DBP initially increased and then decreased (Fig. 1C), indicating that the selectivity and fluorescence quenching ability of the MIP-red-QDs to DBP was greatly affected at extremely high and low pH values. The reason may be ascribed to that strong acids and strong bases will lead to surface defects in the imprinted silicon layer and even ionized imprinted silicon layer (Hou et al., 2016). When the pH stabilized at 8.0, the highest IF value of 2.48 was obtained. Meanwhile, as shown in Fig. 1D, the IF values of the MIP-red-QDs obviously decreased when the salinity exceeded 5 ‰ because of the increase in ion concentration on the surface of the MIP-red-QDs, which further reduced the specific interactions of the MIP-red-QDs to DBP (Kanawi et al., 2016). When the ion concentration was maintained at 5 ‰, the MIP-red-QDs had the highest response to DBP, and the IF value reached 2.81. These results indicated that the specific interaction between DBP and the imprinted cavity mainly depended on ionic interaction and hydrogen bonding in the imprinted cavity. Therefore, under the optimal conditions of ethanol/water (v/v, 7:3), pH of 8, and salinity of 5 ‰, the detection system for specifically recognizing DBP was constructed.

Under the optimal detection system, the stability of the MIP-QDs was evaluated. The fluorescence intensity and IF of the MIP-red-QDs did not evidently change within 5 weeks (Fig. S6), and the RSD was <4.2 %, indicating that the MIP-red-QDs prepared herein had excellent stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching mechanism of the MIP-QDs
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence quenching mechanism of the MIP-QDs was investigated. As shown in Fig. S7A and S7B, the UV absorption spectrum of DBP (226 nm) did not overlap with that of the QDs (345 nm). Therefore, the fluorescence quenching caused by internal filtering effect could be excluded (Liu et al., 2017). Furthermore, the fluorescence emission wavelengths of the MIP-red-QDs, the MIP-blue-QDs, and the GQDs did not overlap with the UV absorption wavelengths of DBP, indicating that fluorescence resonance energy transfer was not the main pathway of fluorescence quenching. As shown in Fig. S7C and S7D, the -C=O bond of the added DBP and the -NH2 bond provided by APTES form a Meisenheimer complex, resulting in the disappearance of DBP's ultraviolet characteristic peak at 226 nm. (Liu et al., 2010; Stanisavljevic et al., 2015). By comparison, when the NIP-QDs were added, the UV absorption and fluorescence intensity of DBP almost did not change. As DBP concentration increased, the fluorescence lifetime of the system decreased from 35.44 ns to 14.81 ns (Fig. S8), indicating that energy transfer was the main reason for fluorescence quenching. Previous studies have confirmed that QDs complete fluorescence emission by returning the excited electrons excited by photons to the ground state (Mehrzad-Samarin et al., 2017; Huang et al., 2019). Therefore, photoinduced electron transfer (PET) was considered the main reason for the fluorescence quenching of the MIP-QDs. Theoretical calculations using the Gaussian 09 program suite revealed that the Lowest Unoccupied Molecular Orbital (LUMO) and the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) of DBP are −1.72 and − 6.11 eV (Fig. S9), respectively (Wang et al., 2021; Zu et al., 2017; Yuan et al., 2017). Using saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as the reference electrode, the LUMO and HOMO of QDs were measured by cyclic voltammetry (Fig. S9). The LUMO and HOMO of blue-QDs were − 2.98 and − 6.55 eV, respectively. The LUMO and HOMO of GQDs were − 3.55 and − 5.12 eV, respectively. The LUMO and HOMO of red-QDs were − 3.45 and − 6.76 eV, respectively (Detailed methods are described in Section 2 of the Supporting Materials). During the detection process, DBP specifically adsorbs into the imprinting cavity of the MIP-QDs through strong interactions, thereby greatly shortening the distance from QDs. Afterward, DBP attaches to the surrounding QDs as an electron donor to provide electrons to the HOMO of QDs. Therefore, the reducer PET process can be considered as the main cause of fluorescence quenching because DBP possesses HOMO (−6.11 eV) between the HOMO and LUMO of the blue-QDs and the red-QDs.

The critical control point in the construction of the TRF sensor was to change the ratio between red, blue, and green (three primary colors) to identify the maximum color abundance. Hence, a red-green dual-emission sensor was firstly optimized before the TRF sensor was constructed. The fluorescence intensity and the color of the GQDs were not affected by DBP, and the fluorescence intensity of the GQDs basically did not change with the extension of DBP addition time (Fig. S10). Therefore, the GQDs could be used as a stable fluorescence internal standard substance. In consideration of its visual recognition ability and economic benefit, the concentration of the GQDs in the system was set to 2 mg/L (10 %). The final concentration of the MIP-blue-QDs and the MIP-red-QDs was 20 mg/L (8 %). As shown in Fig. S11, although the green-emission fluorescence intensity remained basically unchanged, the initial color of the dual-emission sensor changed from light green to olive, corn, and then to bright yellow with the increase in the MIP-red-QDs. The fluorescence of the MIP-red-QDs was quenched with the addition of DBP, and the composite colors of red and green changed. The sensor color varied between light green and olive when the ratio of the MIP-red-QDs was <4 %. The red fluorescence covered the green fluorescence when the ratio of the MIP-red-QDs was over 12 %. The color of the sensor changed between canary and orange, and the color difference was difficult to distinguish. When the proportion of the MIP-red-QDs in the system reached 8 %, the sensor color changed from orange to pale yellow to yellow-green and finally to green (Fig. S11). Moreover, the sensor color still changed from orange to pale yellow when the concentration of DBP was below 5 mg/L compared with the other ratios. Therefore, the ratio of 8 % MIP-red-QDs was selected for the next step of optimization.

The TRF sensor was constructed by adding the MIP-blue-QDs to the above dual-emission system. As the volume proportion of the MIP-blue-QDs (2 %–16 %, VMIP-blue-QDs: VSystem) gradually increased, the original color of the sensor changed from yellow to deep purple (Fig. 2 ). In addition, compared with the dual-emission sensor, the TRF sensor had a higher color resolution and more color types, such as purple and plum. An appropriate MIP-blue-QD volume ratio is important for visual detection. The fluorescence color of the sensor changed from purple to plum to pink to salmon to yellowish green, and finally to green, with the highest degree of richness and differentiation when the ratio of the MIP-blue-QDs was 8 % and when the range of DBP concentration was below 5 mg/L. Therefore, 8 %, 10 %, and 8 % of the MIP-blue-QDs, the GQDs, and the MIP-red-QDs, respectively, were added to the TRF sensor.

The selectivity of the TRF sensor was further evaluated by fluorescence quenching reaction of DBP and its chemical structure analogs DEHP, BBP, DEP, DNP, and DiBP (Fig. S12). As shown in Fig. 3A, DBP had a higher fluorescence quenching efficiency for the MIP-QDs than its analogs. Meanwhile, the fluorescence quenching efficiency of the NIP-QDs was considerably lower than that of the MIP-QDs after the addition of DBP, which was basically the same as that of its structural analogs. These results indicated that the specific imprinting cavities were formed onto the surface of MIP-QDs. As shown in Fig. 3B, the fluorescence quenching efficiency of the TRF sensor after the addition of DBP was substantially higher than those of its analogs and had a more obvious difference than that of the single MIP-QDs. Therefore, the TRF sensor was more sensitive in identifying DBP and its structural analogs than the single MIP-QDs. In addition, the fluorescence quenching efficiency of the TRF sensor based on the NIP-QDs to DBP was considerably lower than that of the MIP-QDs and even slightly lower than that of the structural analogs. The NIP-QDs had a similar ability to recognize DBP and its structural analogs.

Furthermore, the selectivity of the TRF sensor was confirmed by competitive quenching experiments. As shown in Fig. 3C, D. when DBP was mixed with its structural analogs (Cstructural analog = 3CDBP), the structural analogs did not have an obvious influence on the MIP-QDs' specific recognition (Fig. 3C), indicating that the MIP-QDs had an excellent selective recognition ability to DBP. The recognition of DBP by the TRF sensor was basically unaffected, and no remarkable difference was observed in the fluorescence ratio of the sensor to DBP response in the presence of its structural analogs (Fig. 3D). The NIP-QDs based on the TRF sensor also showed irregular changes in fluorescence ratio in the presence of DBP's structural analogs, and their fluorescence ratio was substantially lower than that of the TRF sensor based on the MIP-QDs. Meanwhile, we found that the TRF sensor exhibited a higher IF value (5.47) than that of a single emission sensor (2.81). These results showed that the TRF sensor had excellent selectivity and specificity to DBP. Compared with the single-emission sensor, the TRF sensor had a better selectivity and specificity to DBP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Practical applicability of the developed sensor
ce:section.ce:para:
The potential application in practical samples of the TRF sensor constructed herein was explored by analyzing its linearity, accuracy, precision, limits of detection (LODs), and recoveries for DBP under the optimal system. As shown in Fig. S13, the fluorescence intensity of the MIP-blue-QDs was quenched with the increase in DBP concentration, whereas their F
0 /F value gradually increased and showed an excellent linear relationship within the range of 2.0–20.0 × 103 μg/L. Compared with the MIP-blue-QDs, the NIP-blue-QDs had a lower fluorescence quenching, and the linear relationship between F
0 /F value and DBP concentration was poor, indicating that the NIP-blue-QDs did not have DBP-specific imprinting sites on their surface. The linear formula of the MIP-blue-QDs between the concentration of 2.0 and 20.0 × 103 μg/L was F
0
/F − 1 = 0.0564× + 0.0604 (R2: 0.9976). The LODs, which were calculated as 3σ/S, where S is the slope of the linear equation and σ is the standard deviation of the blank signal, were 0.94 μg/L and 1.4 μg/kg in seawater and fish samples, respectively. As shown in Fig. S14, the fluorescence intensity of the MIP-red-QDs also decreased with the increase in DBP concentration, whereas the fluorescence intensity of the NIP-red-QDs did not evidently change. The linear formula of the MIP-red-QDs between the concentration of 2.0 and 20.0 × 103 μg/L was F
0
/F − 1 = 0.0527× + 0.0492 (R2: 0.9934), and the LODs were 0.86 μg/L and 1.2 μg/kg in seawater and fish samples, respectively.

As DBP concentration increased, the fluorescence intensity of the TRF sensor at 450 and 630 nm substantially decreased, whereas its fluorescence intensity at 530 nm remained basically unchanged (Fig. 4 ). The I RGB value increased with the increase in DBP concentration, showing excellent linear relationship. Meanwhile, the fluorescence color of the sensor changed from purple to plum to pink to orange-red to yellow-green and finally gradually changed to green with the increase in DBP concentration under the irradiation of a 365 nm UV lamp. The linear relationship between I RGB and DBP of the TRF sensor based on the NIP-QDs was poor. The reason may be ascribed to the possibility that the NIP-QDs and the GQDs did not specifically recognize the imprinted site of DBP, and the fluorescence color of the sensor only fluctuated between purple and lavender under the UV lamp. The linear formula of the TRF sensor in seawater and biological samples between the concentration of 2.0 and 20.0 × 103 μg/L was I RGB = 0.0502× + 0.8649 (R2: 0.9910) and I RGB = 0.0329× + 0.8783 (R2: 0.9963), respectively (Fig. S5a and Fig. S15A), and the LODs were 0.65 μg/L and 1.0 μg/kg in seawater and fish samples, respectively. Moreover, the color change of the sensor was still obvious at lower concentrations (0–5 × 103 μg/L). Under UV light, the color of the TRF sensor changed from purple to lavender to orange-red to light yellow to light green.

The sensor was applied to the selective and sensitive detection of DBP in seawater and fish samples. As summarized in Table 1 , the TRF sensor effectively detected DBP in the seawater and biological samples. Intraday and interday RSDs were <10.5 %, and the recoveries were between 84.3 % and 110.3 %. Moreover, different samples showed different fluorescence colors under the UV lamp, such as plum color and lavender, in the actual sample detection. Results demonstrated that the sensor can be successfully used in detecting DBP in real samples. In addition, the detection performance of the constructed sensor was compared with the results of GC–MS/MS (Fig. S15C and S15D). No notable difference was observed between the two methods in detecting DBP in biological and seawater samples, with RSD of <9.4 % (Table 1).

In practical applications, the re-use and reproducibility of sensors must be evaluated. As shown in Fig. S16A, after repeated elution four times, the IF value substantially decreased, indicating that the imprinting cavity of the MIP-QDs was destroyed by repeated elution, which further reduced the ability of the MIP-QDs to recognize DBP (Ahmed et al., 2018). In addition, six batches of identical MIP-QDs were synthesized via the method described in Section 2.2 and tested under optimal conditions. As shown in Fig. S16B, the selectivity of the MIP-QDs to DBP in each batch was basically the same, indicating that the constructed sensor was replicable.

Moreover, the detection performance of the TRF sensor was compared with those reported in the literature (Table S1). The TRF sensor was superior to similar sensors in terms of sensitivity and linear range, and its LOD was as low as one to two orders of magnitude of the same type of sensor to some extent (Zhou et al., 2017; Xu et al., 2017; Lu et al., 2020). The TRF sensor provided a wider range of color variations compared with dual-emission or single-emission sensors (Chen et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2021; Han et al., 2020). Moreover, it had higher imprinting factors and lower LODs than other sensors, which to some extent reduced the error caused by the fluorescence fluctuation of the single-emission fluorescence sensors. Additionally, the visual detection of actual samples was further realized by shortening the reaction time, and the detection efficiency of DBP greatly improved.

In summary, MIP-blue-QDs and MIP-red-QDs were synthesized via an improved reversed microemulsion method. APTES and TEOS were successfully grafted onto the surface of the QDs to achieve highly sensitive and highly selective detection of DBP. Physical and chemical analyses and investigation of the fluorescence quenching mechanism demonstrated that the TRF sensor developed herein for the rapid visual detection of DBP was successfully constructed by optimizing the ratio of the MIP-blue-QDs, the GQDs, and the MIP-red-QDs. Actual sample detection achieved excellent results, and the LOD was as low as 0.65 μg/L. The TRF sensor developed herein further improved the detection efficiency, and it had a better performance in terms of sensitivity and visualization detection than traditional sensors. The TRF sensor developed in this study can be used as an alternative tool for DBP detection to some extent. This ratiometric fluorescence sensor has a broad development prospect and can use extensively in the field of visual detection.

Jin-Jie You: Investigation, Resources, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Hua Liu: Validation, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Rong-Rong Zhang: Supervision, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Qiao-Fen Pan: Investigation, Supervision, Validation. Ai-Li Sun: Investigation, Validation, Supervision. Ze-Ming Zhang: Investigation, Supervision, Validation. Xi-Zhi Shi: Conceptualization, Investigation, Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was funded by the Key Research and Development Program of Zhejiang Province (2022C02028, 2021C02062), the Natural Science Foundation of Zhejiang Province (LGF21C190001, LQ21C200002), Ningbo Science and Technology Bureau (202002N3066, 2022S163) and Major Scientific and Technological Project of Ningbo (2021Z102, 2021Z056).
